summary: Stancil argues that analysis and charts aren’t self-validating the way software or products are, so automating analysis with AI hits a trust wall: you can’t know if the output is correct without recreating it. He uses AI spreadsheet tools and a chart-identification quiz to show that aesthetics and reputation become the only practical validators. In a separate riff, he claims markets now reward “craziness” as a monetizable attention strategy, exemplified by crypto treasury companies.
money_quotes: There’s no way to know if a chart “works” unless you understand how it was built; the validity of the external product depends on the validity of its internals.
You can’t vouch for a spreadsheet without checking all the spreadsheet’s formulas.
If you have to do that, what exactly are we automating here?
For the latter, the inverse may be true—its the aesthetics and benchmarks are what really matter, because we can’t test their work; all we can do is decide if we trust it.
It’s 2025, and in 2025, everything is a meme, and everyone is crazy.
key_insight: Automating analysis runs into a fundamental trust problem because analytical outputs can’t be self-validated, so AI’s gains may shift from correctness to credibility and vibes.
